title: Transposed Convolutions
concept: Transposed convolutions are learnable upsampling layers that allow the network to reconstruct spatial dimensions and details.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main purpose of the decoder in segmentation?

options.text: To upsample the low-resolution features back to the original image size
To reduce the number of channels in the network
To run classifier headers in parallel

exercises.explain: Correct! Decoders restore the spatial resolution.

cards.title: Learnable zoom
cards.body: We know the decoder needs to upsample feature maps. We could use basic math like bilinear interpolation (averaging nearby pixels) to resize. But that's a fixed formula. Wouldn't it be better if the model could learn the best way to fill in the missing details? That's what transposed convolutions do.

cards.title: Transposed Convolution
cards.body: A Transposed Convolution (sometimes called deconvolution) is a learnable upsampling layer. Unlike normal convolutions which reduce size, a transposed convolution uses weights to expand the spatial dimensions of feature maps.

cards.title: Projector lens
cards.body: Imagine a slide projector. A small slide (input feature map) has light shined through a lens (convolutional weights), projecting a much larger and detailed image onto a wall (upsampled output map).

cards.title: Fractional stride
cards.body: By inserting zeros between pixels in a small feature map (e.g., 2x2) and then applying a standard convolution filter, we output a larger map (e.g., 4x4). The filter weights are updated during training.

cards.title: Not 'deconvolution'
cards.body: Many papers call transposed convolutions 'deconvolution'. Mathematically, this is incorrect. A deconvolution reverses the effects of a convolution. Transposed convolution just performs a forward conv in a way that increases size; it does not undo the original math.

left.text:
Fixed mathematical formula
Fast, no weights to learn
Outputs blurry, soft edges

right.text:
Learnable weights & filters
Slower, consumes memory
Outputs sharp, learned patterns

exercises.prompt: Why are transposed convolutions preferred over bilinear interpolation in complex decoders?

options.text: They have learnable parameters that adapt to the dataset during training
They are mathematically simpler and run faster on CPUs
They decrease the spatial dimensions of the image
They do not require activation functions

exercises.explain: Correct! Transposed convolutions learn how to upsample features effectively.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A transposed convolution mathematically reverses a standard convolution's operations.
exercises.explain: False. It only matches the shape change; it does not reverse the mathematical values of a prior convolution.

exercises.sentence: Transposed convolutions are often referred to as fractionally ___ convolutions.
exercises.explain: They are also called fractionally strided convolutions because the stride acts as a fraction to increase size.

exercises.prompt: What does a transposed convolution do to a 2x2 input map?

options.text: It increases the spatial size (e.g. to 4x4)
It flattens it to a 1x4 vector
It reduces it to a 1x1 map

exercises.explain: It upsamples the input to a larger spatial dimension.

exercises.prompt: A common artifact of transposed convolutions is the 'checkerboard artifact' (uneven pixel intensities). What causes this?

options.text: When the kernel size is not divisible by the stride, causing unequal overlap of the filters during upsampling.
Using too many epochs during training.
A lack of training data in the target classes.
Running the model on an incompatible GPU architecture.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If the kernel size doesn't divide evenly by the stride, the overlaps create a checkerboard pattern. Next: how do we fix the loss of fine boundaries using skips?
